title: The Adventure Begins
logline: Keen sails away from home to prove he is ready, loses his way in a storm, and discovers that the love he carries becomes courage when a friend needs him.
declaration: This episode is about the first brave step away from home, and the discovery that growing up does not mean doing it alone. When it ends, the child watching should feel big, brave, and held — like they could try something scary because love can travel with them. When it ends, the parent beside them should feel the ache of letting a child go, and the comfort that the love they gave them becomes the courage the child carries forward. The scene that carries the most weight is the underwater rescue because Keen stops performing bravery and simply chooses someone else, frightened and running out of strength, while his father’s wristbands glow with the truth that he is not alone. The colour of this episode is storm-blue breaking into aquamarine gold. The moment I am most proud of is Keen seeing the wristbands underwater and centering himself before one last pull — because it trusts the child to feel inheritance, fear, love, and courage without explaining them.

theme.declaration: This episode is about the first brave step away from home, and the discovery that growing up does not mean doing it alone. When it ends, the child watching should feel big, brave, and held — like they could try something scary because love can travel with them. When it ends, the parent beside them should feel the ache of letting a child go, and the comfort that the love they gave them becomes the courage the child carries forward. The scene that carries the most weight is the underwater rescue because Keen stops performing bravery and simply chooses someone else, frightened and running out of strength, while his father’s wristbands glow with the truth that he is not alone. The colour of this episode is storm-blue breaking into aquamarine gold. The moment I am most proud of is Keen seeing the wristbands underwater and centering himself before one last pull — because it trusts the child to feel inheritance, fear, love, and courage without explaining them.
theme.theme: Growing up means stepping into the storm with fear still inside you, and discovering that love can become courage when you carry it forward.
theme.leadArc: Keen begins wanting to prove he is ready, capable, and brave on his own; underneath, he needs to understand that real courage is not fearlessness or independence, but acting through fear while accepting that love, family, and help are still with him.
theme.storySpine: Once upon a time, in Crystal Cove, there was a young bear named Keen who wanted to begin his own adventure and prove he was ready. Every day, he tried to be brave by looking certain, checking everything twice, and saying, “I’ve got this.” Until one day, his Mum gave him his father’s wristbands and let him sail away from home. Because of that, Keen crossed open water, met Squeaky, and was caught in a storm that took his map and left him lost. Because of that, he followed Squeaky toward a mysterious light and found that his new friend was trapped and sinking. Until finally, Keen chose courage before he had time to look brave, dove into the storm, saved Squeaky, and discovered the wristbands glowing with the love he carried. And ever since then, Keen has belonged to Crystal Cove not because he never gets scared, but because he knows bravery can have a gulp in it.
theme.promise: By the final frame, the audience must feel that Keen has found a new home without losing the old one — the beach is warmer, the cove is safer, and the adventure ahead feels possible because he is loved.
theme.throughline: The tone is wonder with a lump in its throat: Fuzzby’s full-throttle chaos keeps the episode bright and funny, while Keen’s goodbye, his Mum’s tearful pride, the lost boat, and the father’s wristbands keep the ache present underneath every burst of adventure.
theme.selCompetency: Courage — Keen’s Aquamarine lesson: true courage is not pretending fear is gone, but making the loving choice while fear is still there.
theme.haidt: For the parent, this speaks to the anxious-generation fear of letting children face risk, separation, and uncertainty; the episode quietly argues that children do not become resilient by being kept from storms, but by being lovingly equipped, witnessed, and trusted to act when the world feels too big.
theme.pressureTest: Yes, the truth is universal and earnable here because every major action tests Keen’s performed confidence against real uncertainty: the pier goodbye, the lost map, the storm, the underwater rescue, and the welcome at Crystal Cove. The danger is making courage look like a shiny reward for being fearless, or making the mother’s love too neat and sentimental. The trap every scene must avoid is preaching “be brave” instead of showing the gulp before the choice; the wristbands must feel like carried love, not a magic solution, and the ending must stay bittersweet — Keen belongs, but leaving home still cost something.

beatRule: one 10-12s Seedance take per beat — the beat directs its own internal cuts (director skill v5.0)
_note: Authored by cb_director (Gate 1, BEAT-NATIVE) from the script — 46 beats, 10 scenes.
style: Premium 3D animated feature film aesthetic for children aged 4 to 8, bright hyper saturated colours, warm golden hour sunlight with volumetric rays, glowing magical particles, lighthearted highly expressive slapstick comedy

scenes.name: Rainforest Flowers
scenes.location: A bee-height corridor through an oversized rainforest flower meadow, with towering pollen-heavy blossoms, broad springy leaves, one low crossing branch, and open air lanes between stems for fast flower-to-flower flight.
scenes.weather: Warm breeze, clear sky beginning to carry a distant pressure change.
scenes.lighting: Warm golden morning light with soft pollen scatter; only the faintest coolness begins to creep in at the thunder cue.
scenes.look: Empty establishing plate filled edge-to-edge with tall swaying flowers dominating the frame: large open pollen centers at frame-left, frame-right, and deeper center for repeated dives; a broad flexible leaf angled across the left flight path for a rebound; a small branch crossing the upper midground for a later clip; soft petal surfaces layered below as safe bounce points. Warm morning sunlight streams through drifting pollen, with the far background shadows just beginning to cool at the edges, hinting at distant weather pressure. No characters.
scenes.emotionalCore: Before Keen’s storm arrives, the world feels playful and safe, so the child can laugh first and then feel the weather change under the laughter.
scenes.sceneShotName: Rainforest Flowers — warm morning pollen meadow, bee's-eye wide
scenes.definingFeature: a dense bee-scale tunnel of colossal pollen-heavy flowers with one springy leaf crossing the flight lane
scenes.colorTemperature: warm amber morning safety with faint cool storm-blue edging in the far shadows
scenes.cameraHeight: low bee's-eye looking slightly up through the towering flowers
scenes.ambientBed: A constant warm rainforest-meadow bed: a soft steady breeze moving through tall flower stems, gentle leaf-rustle, faint distant birdsong high in the canopy, and the low continuous hum of bee wings threaded through it — evergreen under every beat, unchanged until the distant thunder rumble arrives as a foreground event layered on top, never replacing the bed.
scenes.parentLine: A parent recognizes their own child in Fuzzby's need to narrate every stumble as a triumph, and feels the quiet relief of watching a friend like Zenny love him without ever needing him to be perfect.

scenes.name: Aida's Sanctuary
scenes.location: A quiet shoreline sanctuary at Crystal Cove where gentle waves meet a smooth sandy-stone shelf, with Aida’s crystal bowl altar beside the water and the forest path rising from the cove edge.
scenes.time: Morning, continuous with the warm start.
scenes.weather: Clear but with distant wind beginning to gather beyond the cove.
scenes.lighting: Soft warm morning gold over the cove, with rose-quartz glow becoming the emotional light during the vision.
scenes.look: Empty warm-morning plate composed around the crystal bowl resting on the shoreline altar in the foreground, its wand placed at the rim ready to be used; gentle waves roll in midground from frame-left across bright blue water, while the forest entrance sits frame-right as the clear path Aida will later take. The cove is open, quiet, and uncluttered, with only soft shoreline stones, low crystal glints, and distant trees shaped by the first hint of gathering wind.
scenes.emotionalCore: Aida feels the shape of a child’s first leaving before anyone names it, and the audience must feel that Keen’s step away from home is already being witnessed.
scenes.sceneShotName: Aida’s shoreline sanctuary — crystal bowl foreground, warm morning wide
scenes.definingFeature: the crystal bowl altar at the water’s edge, positioned between the calm cove and the forest path
scenes.colorTemperature: warm golden morning with rose-quartz tenderness
scenes.cameraHeight: low seated eye-level, looking gently across the bowl toward the cove
scenes.ambientBed: Gentle waves lapping a sandy-stone shoreline in a steady, unhurried rhythm, underscored by a faint, ever-present crystal-bowl resonance and distant birdsong from the rainforest behind; a thin, barely-there thread of gathering wind sits at the very edge of hearing throughout, never rising, never announcing the storm to come.
scenes.parentLine: A parent watching feels the ache of a child's first real leaving mirrored back through Aida's stillness — the relief of trusting a young one to step forward, and the quiet vigilance of staying close enough to help without taking the moment away from them.

scenes.name: The Pier Goodbye
scenes.location: The wooden Crystal Cove pier at the sheltered edge of the beach, with a small loading area beside a neat little sailboat tied to the dock and open sparkling water stretching beyond for the departure.
scenes.weather: Clear and breezy, still safe, with the first hints of open-water wind.
scenes.lighting: Warm golden morning light, bright blue reflections, soft sparkle on water; tenderness under the brightness.
scenes.look: Empty establishing plate with the dock and shore-side loading spot anchoring frame-left, a mooring cleat and rope visible, and the neat little sailboat bobbing beside the dock in the midground, pointed toward open water frame-right. The water path ahead dominates the frame so the boat can push off, wobble, drift away, and later leave room for a dolphin to surface beside it. Clear breezy late-morning light, gentle ripples, rope lines moving slightly in the wind, safe beach warmth behind the dock and wider open-water space ahead; no characters present.
scenes.emotionalCore: Keen wants to look ready, but the goodbye lets the audience feel the ache that love does not stop a child leaving; it gives him something to carry.
scenes.sceneShotName: Crystal Cove pier — warm late-morning goodbye, sailboat moored wide
scenes.definingFeature: the small sailboat tied beside the sunlit goodbye pier, poised between safe shore and open water
scenes.colorTemperature: warm amber safety with a faint open-water blue breeze
scenes.cameraHeight: child-height eye level from the dock, angled slightly toward the open water
scenes.ambientBed: Crystal Cove pier: a steady clear-day breeze moving through rope and sailcloth, small waves lapping the dock posts and hull in a constant soft rhythm, distant open-water hush, with the faint characteristic sparkle-shimmer of the Cove's ambient crystals never audible as a chime, only ever felt as a hush under the water sound.
scenes.parentLine: A parent watching this scene feels the specific ache of equipping a child to leave rather than stopping them — the wristbands, the promise, the wave are all the same gesture: love that travels instead of love that holds on.

scenes.name: At Sea
scenes.location: Endless open water far from Crystal Cove, with a lone small sailboat on a widening blue-grey sea and no land visible anywhere on the horizon.
scenes.weather: Clear blue shifts rapidly into darkening clouds, gusts, rain, then full storm.
scenes.lighting: Blue daylight drains into grey storm light; visibility collapses into rain sheets and cold flashes.
scenes.look: A vast empty ocean dominates the frame, the small sailboat staged left-of-centre with its mast, sail, deck controls and a navigation map visible inside the boat, ready for the action. The horizon stretches wide and low: clearer midday blue lingers on screen-left, while a dark storm front rolls in from screen-right with gust-rippled water, distant rain curtains and rougher waves building in the direction the boat will later turn. Nothing else is present; the frame is built to make the boat feel too small and too far from home.
scenes.emotionalCore: Keen’s performed confidence finally meets real uncertainty, and the audience must feel the stomach-drop of being too far from home without the map.
scenes.sceneShotName: Open sea — incoming storm, small sailboat wide
scenes.definingFeature: the tiny sailboat dwarfed by an incoming wall of grey storm over endless open water
scenes.colorTemperature: cool blue-grey storm fear, with the last clear daylight draining away
scenes.cameraHeight: slightly high looking down from above the waterline
scenes.ambientBed: A constant open-sea bed underneath every beat: water moving against the hull, wind across open water, and the creak of sail rigging — starting as a light breeze-and-lap under clear sky and thickening, without ever cutting out, into driving rain and gusting wind as the storm takes over; the sea itself is always audible, whether as a gentle slosh or a roaring churn.
scenes.parentLine: A parent watches a child's cheerful self-reliance run headlong into something bigger than he is, and feels the particular ache of the moment a kid finally says the true, scary sentence out loud instead of performing bravery through it.

scenes.name: Rainforest Edge
scenes.location: The outer edge of the Whispering Woods where dense rainforest opens toward Crystal Cove’s coast, with wet trees and branches framing a clear sightline out to the stormy sea.
scenes.time: Midday, continuous with the storm building.
scenes.weather: Wind and rain intensify; the storm is now visible offshore.
scenes.lighting: Cool grey-green storm light with occasional pale sky flashes through moving branches.
scenes.look: An empty bee-height stage dominated by wind-lashed wet leaves and swaying rainforest branches in the foreground and midground; the flight path runs left-to-right through the trees toward a coastal gap on screen-right. A single wet branch crosses the upper screen-right path exactly where a fast bee could bonk into it. Beyond the trees, sheets of rain reveal a tiny distant boat rising and falling on dark offshore waves, barely visible through the storm haze. No characters.
scenes.emotionalCore: The comic world sees the danger and turns toward care, so the laughter becomes a doorway into concern rather than an escape from it.
scenes.sceneShotName: Rainforest edge — storm sea gap, bee's-eye wide
scenes.definingFeature: a rain-slashed coastal window through the rainforest, with the tiny boat almost lost beyond it
scenes.colorTemperature: cool blue-grey storm light with muted green rainforest fill
scenes.cameraHeight: bee-height at branch level, looking out through the trees toward the sea
scenes.ambientBed: A constant rainforest-edge storm bed: gusting wind moving through wet leaves and swaying branches, a steady undercurrent of distant surf and offshore rain-hiss, occasional far-off thunder rumbles too low and soft to punctuate any single beat — the sound of a storm gathering just beyond the treeline, never fully arriving.
scenes.parentLine: A parent watching feels the exact moment a running joke about a silly, self-important friend flips into something true: even the goofiest helper stops clowning the second he sees a child might be in danger, and races off imperfectly but without hesitation to get help.

scenes.name: Crystal Cove Beacon
scenes.location: The storm-lashed edge of Crystal Cove where wet sand meets rough open sea, with wind-bent shore grasses behind and a clear foreground crash path leading from beach obstacles toward the waterline.
scenes.weather: Dark clouds, hard wind, rain, rough surf.
scenes.lighting: Storm-blue and grey, cut by warm crystal colour as the beacon forms across the water.
scenes.look: An empty storm shore plate dominated by rough surf and rain-dark sea under heavy midday clouds; wet sand fills the lower frame with a crisp shell and a curved piece of driftwood placed in the foreground crash path, wind-bent grasses line the rear edge of the beach, and the open water stretches outward into rain haze with enough visible distance for a tiny boat to later appear between waves and for a crystal beacon to shoot across the sea. No characters, no boat visible, no extra props.
scenes.emotionalCore: The Cove does not solve Keen’s danger; it sends a light that says he is seen, letting the parent feel witnessed risk and the child feel hope cutting through rain.
scenes.sceneShotName: Crystal Cove shore — storm beacon beach, low wide with crash-path foreground
scenes.definingFeature: the open storm-dark sea framed by a shell-and-driftwood crash path on the wet Crystal Cove shore
scenes.colorTemperature: cool blue-grey storm fear with latent aquamarine hope
scenes.cameraHeight: low shoreline height, slightly below eye level looking out across the wet sand toward the rough sea
scenes.ambientBed: A constant coastal storm bed: steady hard rain hissing on sand and water, a continuous rough-surf rumble breaking and dragging beneath it, and a low gusting wind that keeps the shore grasses moving — present, unchanged in character, under every beat of this scene, swelling only in emphasis where a cut calls for it, never dropping out.
scenes.parentLine: A parent watching feels the specific ache of a caretaker who cannot pull a child out of danger themselves, and finds the only honest response is gathering everyone who loves him and making sure he knows, from however far away, that he has been seen.

scenes.name: Underwater Rescue
scenes.location: Crystal Cove’s storm-churned rescue water just off the beach, spanning the empty bucking boat at the surface, the glowing crystal-lit shore beyond, and the submerged rock-and-drift-net trap below the waves.
scenes.weather: Full storm above, churning current below, massive wave forming at the peak.
scenes.lighting: Darkest storm-blue, cold underwater murk, aquamarine cuff-glow becoming the only steady emotional light, then storm foam and beacon glow on the surface.
scenes.look: An empty split-level waterline plate dominated by violent storm sea: above the surface, a small unmanned boat pitches near frame-left, rain slashes diagonally across towering grey waves, and a distant crystal beam cuts through the storm from the shore; below the surface, blue-grey current reveals a submerged rock near the lower frame with a ragged drift net snagged and streaming in the pull. The glowing shore sits far background as the only hope point, while the massive wave shape gathers overhead, making the ocean feel huge and the rescue space legible.
scenes.emotionalCore: Keen stops trying to look brave and acts while frightened, and the audience must feel love become courage in his body before the crystal ever rewards him.
scenes.sceneShotName: Crystal Cove — storm rescue waterline, shore beacon wide
scenes.definingFeature: the half-submerged view of the ragged drift net caught on a submerged rock beneath a storm wave, with the distant crystal beacon piercing the rain above
scenes.colorTemperature: cold storm blue-grey fear, pierced by faint aquamarine-gold hope from the shore beacon
scenes.cameraHeight: low split-level waterline angle, half above and half below the surface, looking toward the glowing shore
scenes.ambientBed: A constant storm-sea bed: heavy rain hissing across water and wet sand, wind pressing steady and low, distant surf rolling beneath it all, with the crystal beacon's thin steady hum woven underneath throughout.
scenes.parentLine: A parent watching feels the specific ache of a child choosing real danger to save a friend, and the harder discipline of standing back on the shore, trusting him to find his own courage instead of rushing in to fix it for him.

scenes.name: Crystal Cove Welcome
scenes.location: Crystal Cove’s sheltered beach at the waterline, where restless shallow surf meets a broad sweep of wet sand beneath crystal-veined cove walls.
scenes.time: Early afternoon, immediately after the rescue.
scenes.weather: Storm easing; rain thinning; sea still restless but no longer overwhelming.
scenes.lighting: Cool storm light begins warming from behind the clouds; faces and crystals catch soft returning brightness.
scenes.look: An empty, open stage for arrival and welcome: frame-left holds the shallow water and small rolling waves where a dolphin can glide in, the center foreground is broad reflective wet sand with enough clear space for a gathered circle, and frame-right/back curves into the sheltering beach with softly glowing crystals embedded in the cove wall. Rain is thinning into fine mist; the sea still moves but no longer threatens. The composition keeps the waterline readable, the circle-space dominant, and the glowing cove behind it warm and protective. No characters.
scenes.emotionalCore: Keen arrives soaked and shaken into a circle that welcomes him without demanding he be impressive, so belonging feels like being steadied before being celebrated.
scenes.sceneShotName: Crystal Cove beach — rain-thinned welcome shallows, low wide
scenes.definingFeature: the softly glowing aquamarine crystal-veined cove wall sheltering the wet welcome sand
scenes.colorTemperature: cool storm-blue easing into aquamarine-gold safety
scenes.cameraHeight: low child-height at the wet sand, looking slightly toward the shallows and sheltering cove
scenes.ambientBed: Thinning post-storm rain settling to a fine mist, restless-but-easing surf lapping the wet sand in a steady rolling rhythm, faint sea-spray hiss, and the soft ambient shimmer of the crystal-veined cove wall breathing low behind every voice.
scenes.parentLine: A parent watching this scene sees the truest kind of welcome: a frightened, exhausted child is caught, given space, and reassured his mother will know he's safe — before anyone asks him to be impressive or explain himself.

scenes.name: The Aquamarine Gift
scenes.location: The open gathering circle at Crystal Cove after the storm, a smooth rain-darkened stone clearing bordered by crystal-veined greenery, low cove plants, and softly glowing shoreline crystals with enough central space for a ceremony and group embrace.
scenes.weather: Storm passed; fresh air, lingering droplets, clouds breaking.
scenes.lighting: Warm sunlight returns through washed-clean air, with aquamarine glow at the emotional center.
scenes.look: Empty establishing plate with the circular clearing dominating the frame: a clean open center for Aida and Keen, a natural ring of wet stones and low crystals around it where the group can stand, frame-left and frame-right edges kept readable for Fuzzby and Zenny, droplets clinging to leaves and crystal facets, puddle-sheen on the ground reflecting breaking sunlight, storm clouds parting overhead, fresh air and quiet post-storm hush; no characters, no props beyond the circle, wet leaves, stones, and crystals required for the ceremony.
scenes.emotionalCore: Keen receives belonging not as a prize for fearlessness but as recognition that he acted through fear with love still on him.
scenes.sceneShotName: Crystal Cove gathering circle — rain-washed aquamarine-gold wide
scenes.definingFeature: a rain-polished circular stone gathering ring edged with aquamarine-glowing crystals
scenes.colorTemperature: storm-blue clearing into warm aquamarine gold
scenes.cameraHeight: gentle low eye-level looking slightly into the open circle
scenes.ambientBed: Post-storm hush at Crystal Cove: a steady low wash of settling droplets ticking off leaves and crystal facets, faint distant surf beyond the clearing, soft breeze through wet foliage, breaking clouds letting warm air move gently through the circle — constant under every beat of this scene.
scenes.parentLine: A parent watching sees a grieving child being told, without ever saying the word grief, that a new family can hold his old love rather than replace it — recognition and belonging offered without a lesson being spoken aloud.

scenes.name: Honey Friendship Walk
scenes.location: Crystal Cove’s sandy beach path running screen-right beside calm surf, with the cove wall and storm-washed greenery framing a clear walking route for a welcoming group.
scenes.weather: Clear after the storm; calm surf; clean air.
scenes.lighting: Warm golden-hour light, brighter and more settled than the opening, carrying a trace of rain still sparkling on leaves.
scenes.look: Empty post-storm beach plate: calm waves lap along frame-left, a clean damp sand path leads diagonally toward screen-right, and enough open air above the path is left clear for fast bee movement around the walkers. Wet leaves and low beach plants glitter at the path edge, the distant cove sits bright and peaceful, and the whole frame feels newly washed after the storm — warm, safe, and open, with no characters present.
scenes.emotionalCore: The episode releases into laughter without erasing the cost of leaving home, letting Keen’s new family feel joyful while the carried love remains underneath.
scenes.sceneShotName: Crystal Cove beach walk — post-storm golden-hour shoreline, low wide
scenes.definingFeature: the storm-washed golden shoreline path curving beside calm surf into the bright Crystal Cove
scenes.colorTemperature: warm amber golden-hour safety with a faint clean aquamarine after-storm lift
scenes.cameraHeight: low bear-eye height, looking gently along the beach path toward screen-right
scenes.ambientBed: A constant soft wash of calm surf lapping the shore at a gentle, even rhythm, laid under a clean post-storm hush — a faint salt-air breeze moving through wet beach grass and leaves, with the barest occasional glint-tone of distant cove crystals sitting low in the background; no birdsong or wildlife chatter, just water, air and the cove's own quiet shimmer, identical in every beat of this scene.
scenes.parentLine: A parent watching feels the quiet relief of seeing a child who has just been through something frightening get folded into a new community through ordinary, silly warmth rather than a big speech — belonging arriving as play, not ceremony.

beats.storyBeat: Fuzzby and Zenny collect pollen through the tall morning flowers; Zenny works with clean precision while Fuzzby turns the same task into a high-speed flourish, clips a leaf, rebounds, and covers it with proud dignity.
beats.emotionalIntent: The audience feels the meadow as safe, funny, and alive before any danger arrives — a laugh-first opening where Fuzzby’s confidence is lovable, not foolish.
beats.want: Fuzzby wants to look like the fastest, most professional pollen collector in the meadow.
beats.need: Fuzzby needs to be accepted as himself, mess and all, without proving he is perfect.
beats.crystalTruth: No bear crystal is present; the ambient meadow crystals hold a steady warm glimmer that tells the truth underneath Fuzzby’s performance: this is a safe place where imperfection belongs.
beats.kidRead: Fuzzby zooms, bonks a leaf, and pretends he meant it.
beats.adultRead: A child performing competence can still be lovable when the performance collapses; Zenny’s calm presence makes room for him.
beats.theGame: Pollen-collector race where Fuzzby believes style points count more than accuracy, while Zenny quietly plays by the real rules.
beats.physicalFeeling: A whooshing belly-lift as the camera is pulled into Fuzzby’s first fast zig-zag within the opening second.
beats.light: Warm golden morning rays catch pollen clouds around Fuzzby’s path, isolating his speed against Zenny’s steadier silhouette; ambient crystals stay soft and unobtrusive.
beats.atmosphere: Tall petals sway, golden pollen hangs in the air, and the breeze feels gentle enough for comedy to feel safe.
beats.motionTempo: Full-throttle from frame one: chase, scoop, over-exit, FWIP bounce, instant proud snap-back.
beats.grade: Saturated amber-gold with soft green fill, preserving warmth and safety after the leaf impact.

cuts.framing: 24mm wide-angle chase shot at bee height, camera dives between oversized flower stems then whips close behind Fuzzby, with Zenny held frame-right in the same moving world
cuts.action: Tall flowers sway in the warm breeze as Fuzzby and Zenny weave flower to flower collecting pollen; tiny collected pollen puffs bob near their back legs, Zenny working smooth and precise while Fuzzby rockets in a named zig-zag ladder: left petal pass, right stem dodge, low grass skim, loud hum build.
cuts.delivery: fun sing-song rhythm; breathless, delighted, overconfident, the hum rising with speed
cuts.voiceTreatment: voice forward over wing buzz; lyric rhythm locks to the zig-zags

cuts.framing: fast push-in to medium action profile, camera banks with Fuzzby then snaps down to the leaf at impact height
cuts.action: Fuzzby dips low into a flower, scoops up pollen, overdoes the exit, spins sideways into a leaf with a crisp FWIP, rebounds off it in a soft squash-and-spring arc, and straightens in mid-air into a proud superhero chest-out pose as if the leaf were part of the plan.
cuts.delivery: tiny triumphant button; pompous, clipped, absolutely refusing to acknowledge the crash
cuts.voiceTreatment: music ducks for the FWIP and leaves a dry pocket around the line

cuts.framing: cut to locked 50mm medium two-shot, Fuzzby frame-left in proud hover, Zenny frame-right gliding into stillness
cuts.action: Zenny glides up beside him and watches him for one dry beat, her stillness neatly framing his heroic pose while the flowers continue swaying behind them.

beats.cameraArc: The camera behaves like Fuzzby’s overexcited wingman: it chases, banks, drops, and then finally locks only on the proud button and Zenny’s punctuation stare.
beats.pauseHold: One hold only: under 1.2 seconds on Fuzzby’s proud mid-air pose and Zenny’s flat look after “Nailed it.”

performance.surface: Fuzzby performs flawless expertise; Zenny performs calm efficiency.
performance.underneath: Fuzzby knows he nearly lost control but refuses to let dignity fall; Zenny is already fondly unsurprised.
performance.innerThought: Fuzzby: Nobody saw that, therefore it was excellent. Zenny: I saw all of it, and somehow that makes it funnier.

beats.crystalGlow: No bear crystals present; 2–3 ambient meadow crystals remain steady warm, never competing with the action.
beats.startState: Opening frame: Fuzzby is frame-left suspended beside a tall open flower at bee height, chest angled toward the next blossom; Zenny is frame-right beside a neighboring flower, poised neatly with attention on the pollen path. Oversized flowers surround them in warm morning light.
beats.keyframePrompt: Polished 3D CGI animation — modern feature-film CGI (Pixar/DreamWorks quality): fully 3D-modelled characters and environments, physically-based rendering, soft global illumination and volumetric lighting, subsurface scattering on plush fur where applicable, large expressive eyes with warm catch-lights, realistic materials, cinematic depth of field. NOT 2D, NOT hand-drawn, NOT flat. Use the Rainforest Flowers scene plate as reference: oversized flower meadow, tall pollen-heavy blossoms, soft grass paths, swaying leaves, drifting pollen, and a distant opening toward coast-sky only. Characters as per the reference image of Fuzzby and as per the reference image of Zenny, with Fuzzby frame-left and Zenny frame-right. Opening frame only: both already present at bee height beside separate flowers, before the leaf impact. CINEMATOGRAPHY: Lens 24mm wide to make the flower meadow feel immersive and kinetic; camera height at bee-eye level; composition uses tall vertical flower stems with both bees in the upper third; warm golden morning key motivated by sun through canopy from upper-left; cool soft green fill from surrounding leaves; shallow depth of field with foreground pollen bokeh; 2–3 subtle ambient crystals in background bokeh, steady warm.
beats.i2vPrompt: Anchor Image is TRUTH. COPY EXACTLY. Multi-shot sequence with clean cuts between shots. Any airborne bee beats its wings rapidly and continuously; wings rest only when landed. Shot 1 (24mm wide-angle bee-height chase, camera dives between flowers and whips close behind Fuzzby): tall flowers sway in warm breeze; Fuzzby and Zenny weave flower to flower collecting pollen, Zenny smooth and precise in the same frame while Fuzzby rockets through a named zig-zag ladder — left petal pass, right stem dodge, low grass skim — humming louder. FUZZBY: "BIZZY-BIZZY-BIZZY, BIZZY-BIZZY-BIZZY…" Shot 2 (fast push-in to medium action profile, camera banks then snaps to leaf impact): Fuzzby dips low into a flower, scoops pollen, overdoes the exit, spins sideways into a leaf, FWIP, rebounds into the air and instantly straightens into a proud superhero chest-out pose. FUZZBY: "Nailed it." Shot 3 (cut to locked 50mm medium two-shot): Zenny glides up beside him and watches for one dry beat while Fuzzby holds the pose, wings still beating rapidly; flowers continue swaying and meadow ambience resumes. Style+Audio: premium 3D CGI Pixar-style comedy; Seedance scores SFX and timed comic music on the action, voice forward, wing buzz constant, FWIP leaf snap, music duck on the button; no bear note present, use Fuzzby comic buzz motif and Zenny dry ping accent. beats.shot_style: TRUE heart beat: restrained, composed, warm, small-and-real performance; no comedy staging.
beats.director_mode: Pete Docter feeling-first sanctuary vision; Patrick Lin 50mm stillness for emotional weight; Jean-Claude Kalache rose-gold light as story.

beats.performance_notes: Aida must not appear surprised by the vision; she receives it with quiet recognition. The smile is small and knowing, not triumphant. Her line is a decision whispered to herself and the world, not an announcement.
beats.physical_staging_intent: Keep Aida grounded and still until the decision; the only large body change is the graceful rise after the line, making stillness feel earned.
beats.visibility_rule: Only Aida is visible in the opening frame. Keen and Keen's Mum appear only inside the full-frame vision cut. No sanctuary elements are visible during the vision.
beats.contact_rule: Aida’s paw touches her heart only after the vision fades; Keen and his Mum do not touch in the vision, preserving the script’s focus on checking straps and watched worry.
beats.physics_rule: Static props hold position: crystal bowl, shoreline stones, and sanctuary plate do not slide; the wand motion is smooth and small; the vision materialises as light, not as a physical object in the room.
beats.visual_payoff_rule: The beauty moment is the rose-pink vision filling the entire frame and dissolving back into Aida’s held close-up.
beats.failed_correction_rule: Do not add extra dialogue, crystal calls, prophecy gestures, storm flashes, or any explanatory narration.

beats.endState: Aida rises from the shoreline altar with unhurried grace, her paw only just leaving her heart, the last of the rose glow settling back to a steady warm pink in her pendant; her smile stays small and knowing, not triumphant, as she turns her body fully away from the bowl and starts into the forest, already mid-stride, her weight and gaze both committed forward.

beats.need: Keen needs to let his Mum see that leaving feels big, even if he can still go.

beats.adultRead: A parent sees the child over-controlling the small things because the big thing cannot be controlled.
beats.theGame: The Ready-Check Game: if every pouch, blanket, and map sits perfectly, Keen can pretend his tummy is not wobbling.
beats.physicalFeeling: A warm chest-squeeze in the first two seconds, like holding a breath before a goodbye.
beats.light: Warm late-morning amber isolates Keen and the boat, with soft water sparkle wrapping Mum as the home anchor.
beats.atmosphere: Clean sea breeze moves the ropes and sailcloth gently; fine salt glints in the air, safe but already open and wide.

beats.storyBeat: The wind hardens around the rainforest edge as Fuzzby and Zenny fly back through the trees; Fuzzby chatters ahead until he stops dead, points through the rain, and sees a tiny boat barely visible in the storm.
beats.emotionalIntent: The laugh catches in the audience’s throat: Fuzzby’s comic momentum turns into real alarm without losing his lovable ridiculousness.
beats.want: Fuzzby performs confidence by narrating the storm like he is in charge of it; Zenny wants to keep moving efficiently and read the situation clearly.
beats.need: Fuzzby needs to let the danger matter more than his performance; Zenny needs to let her dry composure soften into care.
beats.crystalTruth: No crystal-bearing bear is in frame; the truth is carried by the environment: tiny ambient crystals at the rainforest edge dim under cool grey-green storm light, contradicting Fuzzby’s breezy chatter and revealing that the moment is no longer just funny.
beats.kidRead: Fuzzby is zooming along talking too much, then freezes so suddenly Zenny almost bumps him.
beats.adultRead: The instant the tiny boat appears, the comedy becomes concern: a child is out beyond help, and even the jokester knows it.
beats.theGame: Storm-spotting relay: the made-up rule is that the fastest flyer must stop being silly the second the forest shows someone needs help.
beats.physicalFeeling: A sudden held breath as forward motion cuts off in the first two seconds.
beats.light: Cool grey-green storm light slices through the tree gap and isolates the distant water, while the bees remain rimmed by the last warm pollen-gold from the forest behind them.
beats.atmosphere: Wind-driven rain, shivering leaves, and misted sea spray build from playful motion into a thin veil that parts just enough to reveal the tiny boat.
beats.motionTempo: Fast tracking flight slams into a complete mid-air stop, then drops into slow, careful pointing and a short held look.
beats.grade: Desaturated storm green-blue with a faint warm edge behind the bees, preserving the feeling that safety is behind them and danger is ahead.

cuts.framing: wide bee-height tracking shot, 18mm, low through wind-bent branches, camera chasing from behind-left then sliding beside Fuzzby
cuts.action: The wind picks up around the trees. Leaves shiver. Branches sway harder now. Fuzzby and Zenny move quickly through the air, heading back through the forest. Fuzzby flies slightly ahead — still talking, because of course he is. Both airborne bees keep their wings beating rapidly and continuously.
cuts.dialogue: FUZZBY: I’m just saying, if this turns into a full storm situation, I am extremely—
cuts.delivery: Manic, self-important, breathless patter; he is mid-boast and fully believes he is about to sound impressive.

cuts.framing: snap-in two-shot, 35mm, camera brakes late with a tiny overshoot and settles at bee eye level
cuts.action: He stops dead in mid-air. Completely still. Zenny nearly bumps into him, checking herself just short of contact. Wings continue rapid fluttering to hold them in place.
cuts.delivery: Dry, small, puzzled; almost no movement except the tiniest head tilt.

cuts.framing: over-Fuzzby’s-pointing-paw shot through the coastal gap, 50mm compressed by rain, slow push toward the grey sea
cuts.action: No response. Fuzzby slowly raises one tiny paw… Points. Zenny follows his gaze. Out beyond the trees. Through the shifting wind and rain, a tiny boat rises and falls in the distance. Barely visible in the storm. Beat. Fuzzby’s eyes go WIDE.

cuts.framing: tight close-up on Fuzzby, 85mm, rain streaks crossing foreground, Zenny held soft on frame-right edge
cuts.action: Fuzzby stays frozen in mid-hover for one stunned beat, eyes wide at the distant boat, his earlier bravado gone quiet but not made sentimental.
cuts.dialogue: FUZZBY: …that is NOT a good place to be.
cuts.delivery: Low, stunned, genuinely worried; the line lands after the visual beat, not over it.

beats.cameraArc: The camera begins as a chasing comic companion, brakes with Fuzzby as if surprised by him, then becomes still and intent through the rain as the scene discovers the danger.
beats.pauseHold: Hold on the distant boat and Fuzzby’s widened eyes for about 1.2 seconds before his line; it is the scene’s quiet catch, not a dead stop for sentiment.

Shot 3 (cut to over-Fuzzby’s-pointing-paw shot through the coastal gap, 50mm compressed by rain, slow push toward the grey sea): no response; Fuzzby slowly raises one tiny paw and points; Zenny follows his gaze; out beyond the trees, through shifting wind and rain, a tiny boat rises and falls in the distance, barely visible in the storm; hold the beat as Fuzzby’s eyes go wide. Shot 4 (cut to tight close-up on Fuzzby, 85mm, rain streaks crossing foreground, Zenny soft on frame-right edge): Fuzzby remains frozen in mid-hover for one stunned breath, wings still beating fast, then speaks quietly. FUZZBY: "…that is NOT a good place to be." Zenny hovers just behind and beside him on frame-right, gaze following the same line, her posture softened from her usual dry stillness into something more alert and caring, wings holding her steady in the wind.

beats.carryMarks: Fuzzby's paw still raised from the point; both bees' wide, alert expressions
beats.actingContrast: Fuzzby's manic chatter snaps into total stillness while Zenny's habitual dry composure is the one thing that visibly softens — his motion stops as her guard drops, the two contrasts crossing in the same instant.
beats.emotionMechanic: The beat plays Fuzzby's want (performing confidence) against his need (letting the danger matter) by physically stopping his motion dead the instant the boat appears — the feeling itself is the mechanic, since it is the abrupt loss of comic momentum, not any dialogue, that tells the audience the crystal truth: this stopped being just funny.

beats.storyBeat: Zenny names the danger, Fuzzby snaps into emergency action, blasts toward the beach, immediately bonks into a branch, shakes it off, and keeps going while Zenny follows faster and focused.
beats.emotionalIntent: The audience laughs with relief because Fuzzby’s panic is ridiculous, but the direction of the joke is care: he is racing to get help.
beats.want: Fuzzby wants to become the heroic emergency messenger instantly and look decisive; Zenny wants to keep the rescue focused and efficient.
beats.need: Fuzzby needs to accept that helping matters more than looking smooth; Zenny needs to move faster than her usual deadpan pace because this time the danger is real.
beats.crystalTruth: No crystal-bearing bear is in frame; ambient crystals along the branch line flicker faintly as the storm pressure rises, revealing urgency beneath Fuzzby’s heroic pose.
beats.kidRead: Fuzzby yells for the bears, rockets off, bonks into a branch, then pretends the bonk did not happen.
beats.adultRead: The comic crash does not undercut the danger; it shows that even imperfect helpers can become the first link in a rescue.
beats.theGame: Emergency relay: the rule is simple and child-readable — see danger, shout for help, go now, even if you bonk first.
beats.physicalFeeling: A jolt of forward panic as Fuzzby launches before his body is ready.
beats.light: Cold storm light flashes across the branch he is about to hit, isolating the obstacle for a split second before the BONK; Zenny stays cleanly readable in softer side light behind him.
beats.atmosphere: Rain streaks, whipping leaves, and wing-buzz accelerate around Fuzzby’s launch; the air snaps back into storm noise after the branch impact.
beats.motionTempo: Full throttle from frame one: softening beat, snap, rocket launch, late brake, BONK, recoil, shake-off, immediate heroic continuation.
beats.grade: Cool storm blue-green with sharp wet highlights on leaves and bark, preserving the urgency while allowing the branch bonk to read crisply.

beats.actingContrast: Zenny's plain, immediate naming of the danger is the calm fuse that lights Fuzzby's instant over-the-top eruption into heroic panic — her economy of words against his explosive triple-shout is the beat's whole comic engine, and neither one breaks character to acknowledge the other's extremity.

beats.storyBeat: The bears stand on the storm shore as Fuzzby arrives too fast, fails to stop, pinballs through the beach obstacles, lands in a soggy heap, and covers with pride.

beats.actingContrast: Fuzzby's loud, undented pride plays directly against the bears' flat, wordless non-reaction — his performance needs an audience that refuses to give him one.

beats.storyBeat: Fuzzby scrambles up and tries to deliver the warning, runs out of breath, and Zenny arrives steady enough to finish the only words that matter.
beats.emotionalIntent: The joke turns into clarity: the child laughs at Fuzzby’s broken message, while concern becomes unmistakable.
beats.want: Fuzzby wants to be the loud, important bearer of the emergency.
beats.need: He needs help finishing the truth so the Cove can act.

beats.kidRead: Fuzzby can’t get the sentence out, and Zenny solves it in two words.
beats.adultRead: In a crisis, love sometimes looks like someone calmly naming what panic cannot finish.
beats.theGame: Emergency Charades: Fuzzby can only flap, point and run out of words; Zenny wins by saying the tiny true thing.
beats.physicalFeeling: A breathless chest-squeeze as the warning gets stuck in Fuzzby before Zenny releases it.

cuts.framing: medium low 35mm on Fuzzby with the bears behind, handheld micro-jitter from storm wind

cuts.delivery: Huge grateful confirmation, too loud because he is pretending the stumble was part of the briefing.
cuts.voiceTreatment: bright panic, clipped consonants

performance.underneath: Fuzzby is frightened by the boat and needs someone calmer to carry the sentence; Zenny’s care shows as precision, not fuss.
performance.innerThought: Fuzzby: Say it fast enough and it won’t be scary. Zenny: The scary thing needs fewer words.

continuity.opensFrom: Fuzzby has just landed in a soggy heap and covered with “I meant to do that” while nobody reacted.
continuity.carryToNext: The message is now clear: someone is in trouble offshore. Zenny has entered and remains frame-right; Fuzzby is upright and pointing toward her.

check.focalSubject: Fuzzby’s failed warning becoming Zenny’s exact diagnosis.
check.emotionalRead: Relief that the chaos has finally named the danger.
check.heartCheck: The child laughs at the stammer-and-finish; the parent feels the mercy of calm words during panic.

opensOn.action: scrambling up out of the soggy heap, wild-eyed, already trying to force the emergency out before he's fully on his feet

beats.actingContrast: Fuzzby's manic, air-starved stammering plays against Zenny's flat, economical two-word precision — his volume needs her stillness to actually land the message.
beats.emotionMechanic: Fuzzby's want (to be the important messenger) outrunning his own lungs is the mechanic: he needs Zenny's calm precision to finish what his panic can't, so the laugh at his broken sentence and the relief at her plain truth arrive in the same breath.

beats.storyBeat: Aida steps to the water’s edge, still in the wind, sees the tiny boat through the rain, and turns the group from concern into coordinated action.
beats.emotionalIntent: The audience holds its breath: the danger is real, but being seen changes the feeling from helplessness to hope.
beats.want: Aida wants to keep the group steady and turn fear into a shared response.
beats.need: She needs to let the Cove’s love reach outward without pretending it can control the storm.
beats.crystalTruth: Aida’s rose quartz remains steady-warm before activation, contradicting the severity of the storm: her heart knows the truth is not panic, it is presence. Keen is far away with vacant wristbands, seen but not yet reached.
beats.kidRead: Aida spots the tiny boat and tells everyone to use their crystals.
beats.adultRead: A caretaker cannot remove the risk, but she can gather witnesses and send a signal that the child is not alone.
beats.theGame: Beacon Rules: when someone is too far to touch, everyone gathers close and makes a looking-light; the light cannot pull the storm away, only say, “we see you.”
beats.physicalFeeling: A held breath as the tiny boat appears between rain sheets.
beats.light: The storm grade cools darker at the edges while a faint rose warmth shapes Aida’s face and pendant, pulling the eye to her stillness at the waterline.
beats.atmosphere: Rain curtains open and close over the sea like breathing; wind moves Aida’s surroundings while she remains almost motionless.
beats.motionTempo: The tempo drops from Fuzzby’s staccato into Aida’s measured step, long look, quiet identification, group look-out, decisive turn, and closing gather.
beats.grade: Cool storm-blue with restrained rose warmth, preserving worry and hope in the same frame.

cuts.framing: tracking medium-wide 35mm from behind Aida’s shoulder, slow forward move to the water’s edge
cuts.action: Aida steps forward to the water’s edge. The wind stirs around her, but she remains still and looks out across the stormy sea; the others hold behind her, with Fuzzby frame-left and Zenny frame-right.

cuts.action: Through the rain we glimpse a tiny boat rising and falling between waves; Keen is only a distant rain-obscured presence inside the boat, with no readable wristband detail.
cuts.delivery: Soft certainty, not alarm; she has found him with her heart before the group can fully see him.
cuts.voiceTreatment: quiet but clear above rain

cuts.framing: cut to 50mm medium on Aida turning back to the group, camera locked as the group fills behind her
cuts.action: The bears all look out. Concern and hope cross the group together. Aida turns to them, and the bears gather closer around her call.
cuts.dialogue: AIDA: Everyone — activate your crystal powers.
cuts.delivery: Measured command with warmth underneath; no panic, no shout, the authority comes from stillness.
cuts.voiceTreatment: warm, centered, voice forward

beats.pauseHold: A 1.2-second held breath on the rain-compressed glimpse of the tiny boat before Aida says “There he is.”

performance.surface: Aida is calm, precise and quietly commanding; the group shifts from scattered concern into readiness.
performance.underneath: Aida knows fear is present, but she refuses to let it become the leader; the others need her steadiness to find their own.
performance.innerThought: Aida: We cannot pull him from here, but we can make sure he knows he is seen. Fuzzby: Please let them understand how bad this is. Zenny: Now we do the useful thing.

opensOn.action: already stepping forward past the group toward the water's edge, the others watching her go

beats.actingContrast: Aida's measured, unhurried stillness plays against the group's held, open concern behind her — her quiet is what lets their worry turn into readiness instead of panic.
beats.emotionMechanic: Aida's need to offer presence rather than false control is the beat's mechanic: she cannot close the distance to Keen, so the feeling itself — steady, unpanicked care — becomes the action that turns the group from scattered fear into a shared response.

beats.storyBeat: The group gathers close for the Crystal Call; Aida’s rose quartz glows, the others’ crystals pulse one by one, and a brilliant combined light cuts through the storm-dark sea like a beacon.
beats.emotionalIntent: The audience feels warmth push through cold rain: the Cove cannot rescue Keen yet, but it can send him proof that he is seen.
beats.want: Aida and the group want to reach Keen across distance without pretending the danger is gone.
beats.need: They need to offer connection, not control: a light that guides and witnesses rather than fixes.
beats.crystalTruth: Aida’s face is composed, but her rose quartz glows warmly with the need beneath it: love must move outward. The others’ crystals brighten one by one, revealing shared care before the group says it aloud.
beats.kidRead: Everyone touches their crystals, colours build together, and a huge magical light shoots across the storm.
beats.adultRead: A community cannot remove a child’s storm, but it can become a beacon that says, “you are not alone out there.”
beats.theGame: Beacon Rules: each crystal adds one colour to the looking-light; no single bear owns it, and it only works when everyone lends their glow together.
beats.physicalFeeling: A warm chest-bloom as colour gathers against cold rain and releases across the sea.
beats.light: The dominant storm-blue is pierced first by Aida’s rose warmth, then by layered crystal colours, until the combined beam becomes the motivated key light across faces, rain and waves.
beats.atmosphere: Rain turns into sparkling streaks as the beam forms; sea mist catches the colours and the storm briefly gains depth instead of darkness.
beats.motionTempo: Slow ritual touch, pulse-by-pulse brightening, unified spoken call, then a fast decisive burst that travels outward in one clean direction.
beats.grade: Storm-blue breaking into saturated crystal glow, preserving the cold edges so the beacon feels earned and necessary.

beats.storyBeat: The crystal beacon reaches Keen through the storm; hope lifts him for one breath before Squeaky is pulled under by the drift net, and Keen chooses the water without hesitation.
beats.emotionalIntent: The audience feels the sudden drop from hope to danger, then the gulp before a brave choice.
beats.want: Keen wants to keep steering the crisis from the boat and prove he can handle it.
beats.need: He needs to stop performing readiness and choose help while fear is still in his body.
beats.crystalTruth: Keen’s face tries to jump to action, but the vacant wristbands sit dark and heavy until the choice begins; they hold the need for carried love before any visible glow.
beats.kidRead: Keen sees magic light, then his dolphin friend needs saving, so he jumps in.
beats.adultRead: A child who just left home is forced into real risk; courage begins before he feels ready.
beats.theGame: Storm Rescue Rules: when the light appears, follow it; when a friend sinks, the rule changes from staying safe to staying with them.
beats.physicalFeeling: A stomach-lurch as the boat bucks and Squeaky drops out of sight.
beats.light: Cold blue-grey storm light swallows the deck while the distant crystal beam cuts a thin aquamarine line across Keen’s face, isolating the instant hope becomes responsibility.
beats.atmosphere: Rain sheets, spray, and wind blur the horizon; the beam’s particles arrive clean and steady against the chaotic water.
beats.motionTempo: Hope lifts slowly for one beat, then the boat snaps, Squeaky cries, and Keen’s decision lands in one clean plunge.
beats.grade: Dark storm-blue with a narrow aquamarine highlight preserved on Keen and the waterline.

beats.light: Warm post-storm sunlight isolates Aida and Keen at the center while aquamarine shimmer becomes the only saturated color between them.
beats.atmosphere: Washed-clean air, sparkling droplets, and faint crystal motes gather inward toward the space between Aida and Keen.
beats.motionTempo: Slow, reverent, floating; the only active motion is the light forming and finding its home.
beats.grade: Aquamarine-gold after-rain warmth, preserving the tenderness without becoming sugary.

cuts.framing: Low 35mm wide establishing, gentle slow push-in through sparkling droplets toward the quiet circle
cuts.action: The storm has passed; everything glows fresh and washed clean. Droplets cling to leaves and sparkle in sunlight. The Crystal Bears gather in a quiet circle with Aida and Keen at the center; Keen still wears his father’s wristbands, and Aida looks at him with warmth and certainty.
cuts.dialogue: AIDA: You followed your heart… and chose courage.
cuts.delivery: Warm, measured, certain; quiet authority, not ceremony voice.
cuts.voiceTreatment: Final ElevenLabs V3 supplied as @Audio1; lip-sync exact locked line.

cuts.framing: 50mm medium two-shot at center height, locked for emotional weight
cuts.action: A gentle Aquamarine light begins to shimmer in the air between Aida and Keen; a glowing crystal appears as Keen stares in wonder.

cuts.framing: 85mm intimate insert moving from the hovering crystal to Keen’s wrists, then tilting up to his eyes
cuts.action: The crystal lifts, humming softly, splits into two smaller gems, and the gems float toward Keen’s wrists; one settles into each wristband perfectly. A pulse of azure light runs through them; Keen feels it instantly, lifts his paws amazed, and the light reflects in his eyes.

beats.cameraArc: The camera begins as the whole circle witnessing him, then narrows until the only thing that matters is the light choosing the wristbands and Keen’s eyes receiving it.
beats.pauseHold: Hold 1.2 seconds on Keen’s amazed eyes after the azure pulse reflects there.

performance.surface: Keen stands careful and small inside the attention, trying to be composed; Aida is steady and sure.
performance.underneath: Keen cannot yet believe that what he did counts as courage; Aida already knows it does.
performance.innerThought: Keen: Did they really see me? Aida: Let him feel it before anyone celebrates it.

beats.crystalGlow: Aida’s Rose Quartz remains steady warm; the Aquamarine light brightens from shimmer to formed crystal, then transfers into Keen’s wristbands as a clean azure pulse.

check.focalSubject: Keen receiving the Aquamarine truth through the wristbands.
check.emotionalRead: Recognition lands softly before anyone cheers.
check.heartCheck: The child feels magical wonder; the parent feels a child’s inherited love being honored by a new community.

beats.storyBeat: Keen speaks the Aquamarine Crystal Call with rising confidence, and the newly set wristbands glow brighter as the energy settles warmly around him.
beats.emotionalIntent: The audience feels confidence arrive as surrender, not performance — a small voice becoming steady because it is supported.
beats.want: Keen wants to say the call strongly enough to deserve the light.
beats.need: Keen needs to let the light answer the truth he already lived, without forcing it.
beats.crystalTruth: Keen’s face begins as effortful and awed; the crystal-set wristbands brighten steadily, revealing that his courage is already inside him and does not need to be shouted into existence.
beats.kidRead: Keen says the special Aquamarine words and his wristbands glow brighter.
beats.adultRead: This is not a reward for fearlessness; it is a child accepting that love and fear can live inside courage together.
beats.theGame: The Call-and-Glow Rule: the light gets brighter only when the words come from the real place, not the loud place.
beats.physicalFeeling: A spark running from the wrists up through the arms, then settling warm around the ribs.
beats.light: Aquamarine light from Keen’s wristbands becomes the key light, wrapping his paws and face while the group stays in soft gold fill.
beats.atmosphere: Fine mist and droplet motes catch the azure pulse, then calm as the energy settles.
beats.motionTempo: Breath-led: tiny inhale, steady line, glow swell, warm settle.
beats.grade: Saturated aquamarine over warm post-rain gold, with shadows kept soft and safe.

cuts.action: Keen holds his lifted paws near his chest, amazed by the newly set wristbands; he gathers himself and lets the call rise with confidence.
cuts.dialogue: KEEN: Brave and bright, with all my might — Aquamarine Crystal, shine with light!
cuts.delivery: with rising confidence; sincere, breath-supported, not shouted.
cuts.voiceTreatment: Final ElevenLabs V3 supplied as @Audio1; lip-sync exact locked line.

cuts.framing: Extreme close insert on both wristbands, slow controlled push-in
cuts.action: His wristbands glow brighter with a clean azure pulse that blooms from the Aquamarine gems.

cuts.framing: Low 35mm widening two-shot into group circle, gentle dolly out
cuts.action: The energy settles warmly around Keen; Aida remains steady beside him and the circle receives the glow in quiet wonder.

beats.cameraArc: A locked close-up lets Keen carry the sincerity; the camera then follows the light down to the wristbands and opens back out as the warmth reaches the circle.
beats.pauseHold: Hold 1 second after the glow settles around Keen before the next beat’s comic punctuation.

performance.surface: Keen speaks with rising confidence and wonder.
performance.underneath: He is still a little overwhelmed, but he lets himself be held by the call instead of pushing it.
performance.innerThought: Keen: I don’t have to make this brave. I can let it be true.

beats.storyBeat: The storm has lifted into a golden beach walk; Fuzzby buzzes up beside Keen with absurd seriousness and discovers Keen likes honey, which to Fuzzby means instant lifelong friendship potential.
beats.emotionalIntent: The audience feels the body-warm release after danger: Keen is still breathless from the storm, but the new family is already making room for him through play.
beats.want: Keen wants to keep up and seem okay after everything; Fuzzby wants to make the friendship official through honey protocol.
beats.need: Keen needs to feel he can be tired, new, and welcomed without performing bravery; Fuzzby needs to belong by sharing what he loves without overwhelming the moment.
beats.crystalTruth: Keen’s face says he is laughing it off, but his crystal-set wristbands hold a steady-warm-but-changed aquamarine glow, revealing that the love from home is still with him inside this new welcome.
beats.kidRead: Fuzzby asks the most serious question in the world and treats honey like destiny.
beats.adultRead: A child who has just left home is being folded into a new circle gently, through ordinary silliness rather than speeches.
beats.theGame: Honey Friendship Rules: if the new friend likes honey, the friendship can begin immediately, but only if everyone keeps walking together.
beats.physicalFeeling: A warm unclenching in the chest as the storm releases into laughter within the first two seconds.
beats.light: Low golden-hour sun rims the walking group from the sea side while aquamarine glints from Keen’s wristbands briefly catch the lens, isolating the relief inside the comedy.
beats.atmosphere: Clean post-storm air, tiny rain droplets still sparkling on beach grass, soft salt mist and a few drifting crystal motes build a feeling of washed-clean safety.
beats.motionTempo: A buoyant walking rhythm under Fuzzby’s fast, precise hover darts: rocket in, brake too close, hover-serious, then explosive delight.
beats.grade: Warm amber-gold with a faint aquamarine undertone preserved in shadows, keeping the scene joyful but still carrying the memory of the storm.

Shot 2 (cut to wide-angle orbiting two-shot around Keen, 18mm, camera chases Fuzzby’s exact circular path once around Keen): Fuzzby is already buzzing excitedly around Keen; he rockets from Keen’s left shoulder to above his head, loops once around him, points toward imaginary honey stations in the air, keeps clear of contact, and says FUZZBY: "Honey cakes, honey snacks, honey naps, honey leaf drizzles, emergency honey—" Shot 3 (snap cut to clean side two-shot, 35mm): Zenny gently catches one of his legs and pulls him back in one smooth backward tow, stopping the orbit; Fuzzby keeps a proud face as if this restraint was planned, and Zenny says ZENNY: "One thing at a time." Shot 4 (dynamic medium on Fuzzby foreground with Keen and the laughing group behind, then slow crane-pull to the wide peaceful cove, 24mm): Fuzzby instantly recovers into a chest-out heroic hover and says FUZZBY: "I have waited my whole life for this friendship!" Hold the button for 1.2 seconds; Keen laughs, the others laugh too as they continue walking together down the beach, and the camera lingers on the cove, bright and peaceful now. Style+Audio: premium 3D CGI Pixar/DreamWorks quality; Seedance scores wave lap, soft sand steps, fast wing-buzz orbit, tiny elastic leg-catch brake, Zenny dry punctuation, warm group laughter, and a gentle golden-aquamarine closing musical lift around Keen’s B note and the family chord; voice forward for lip-sync; no text, no watermarks, no logos. Duration 15 seconds. Ratio 16:9.
beats.soundIntent: Start with soft waves and walking sand; music ducks almost out for Zenny’s dry “Fuzzby…”; Fuzzby’s orbit gets a rising wing-buzz Doppler and tiny honey-menu percussion pops; Zenny’s leg-catch lands with a soft elastic twip, not a hurt sound; final declaration gets a proud brass-like comic swell that melts into warm group laughter, wave lap, and Keen’s aquamarine B resolving into a gentle family chord.

continuity.opensFrom: Continues directly from Fuzzby’s “VERY well” button, with the group still walking screen-right and Zenny positioned to rein him in.
continuity.carryToNext: No next beat; the camera lingers on Crystal Cove bright and peaceful as the episode fades out, with Keen walking inside the group and Fuzzby contained but delighted.

check.focalSubject: Fuzzby’s honey frenzy framed by Zenny’s gentle containment and Keen’s laughter.
check.emotionalRead: The final laugh feels safe, earned, and still a little tender from the storm that came before.
check.heartCheck: The child feels the joy of a silly new friendship; the parent feels the ache ease as Keen is held by a community without losing the love he brought from home.

beats.shot_style: Fuzzby chase-orbit comedy resolving into beauty exit wide
beats.gag_carry: Fuzzby escalates from honey menu to lifelong friendship declaration; Zenny’s pullback provides the brake and the frame for the joke.
beats.director_mode: final-ripple-comedy-with-beauty-release
beats.audience_feeling_target: buoyant laughter settling into warm belonging
beats.emotional_function: counter + action + climax + reaction-hold + exit wide
beats.script_truth_lock: Zenny warns; Fuzzby buzzes excitedly around Keen listing honey plans; Zenny catches one leg and pulls him back; Fuzzby declares lifelong friendship; Keen and the others laugh and the camera lingers on the peaceful cove.
beats.music_emotion: comic sprint into warm golden-aquamarine closing chord
beats.performance_notes: Zenny must not yank harshly; the pullback is gentle and affectionate. Fuzzby never acknowledges being restrained as failure — he recovers instantly into heroic sincerity.
beats.physical_staging_intent: Stage one precise Fuzzby orbit around Keen, one gentle leg-catch brake by Zenny, one heroic declaration button, then release to group laughter and cove beauty.
beats.visibility_rule: All scene characters remain present in the beach walk; Fuzzby stays frame-left energy, Zenny frame-right deadpan counterbalance whenever they share frame.
beats.contact_rule: Only Zenny gently catches one of Fuzzby’s legs and pulls him back; no other contact is added.
beats.physics_rule: Fuzzby’s orbit is specific and readable: rocket, loop once, point, get caught, tow back, recover; airborne wings flap continuously; Zenny’s pull changes his path without deformation or harm.
beats.visual_payoff_rule: The final finisher is Fuzzby’s proud friendship declaration and the lingering peaceful cove, not the leg catch itself.
beats.failed_correction_rule: Fuzzby must not look embarrassed or defeated after Zenny pulls him back; he instantly treats it as part of his grand announcement.
beats.continuity_physical_rule: Keen’s crystal-set wristbands persist; the beach remains post-storm calm with no lost boat, no ceremony circle, and no new props.
beats.prohibited_staging: Do not add a crash, bonk, leaf hit, extra honey props, or extra dialogue; do not let the final shot erase the rain-sparkle trace of the storm.
beats.endState: Fuzzby snaps out of Zenny's gentle tow into a full chest-out heroic hover again, wings beating fast and proud, declaring his friendship with total unironic conviction; Keen breaks into real laughter beside him, and the rest of the group laughs along as they keep walking screen-right; the camera cranes slowly back and up, releasing off the characters to hold on the wide, bright, peaceful cove, waves still lapping calmly below.
beats.endStateStill: Fuzzby in a chest-out heroic hover, proud and beaming, close beside Keen who is laughing openly. Zenny nearby, dry and settled, having just released his leg. The rest of the group walking together behind them, all smiling. Wide shot opening out to the calm, sunlit Crystal Cove beyond, waves lapping at frame-left, wet leaves glittering, golden-hour light over the whole beach.
beats.carryMarks: Fuzzby's heroic chest-out hover pose beside a laughing Keen, cove opening wide behind them

opensOn.action: watching Fuzzby's excitement tip past the point she can ignore, drawing a slow breath before her flat warning

beats.actingContrast: Zenny's dry, near-motionless containment plays directly against Fuzzby's unstoppable orbiting escalation — her stillness is the brake that lets his chaos read as funny instead of overwhelming, and Keen's open laughter is the proof the balance is working.
beats.emotionMechanic: The scene's biggest comic engine — Fuzzby's runaway honey-menu escalation, Zenny's gentle physical brake, and his instant recovery into sincerity — is what lets the episode's final feeling land as safe: the need (Keen belonging without losing what he carries from home) resolves through laughter that includes him rather than a speech directed at him, so the crystalTruth's steady glow reads as truth rather than a lesson.

northStarAnswers:
NOTE ON SOURCING: Canon (§0 NORTH STAR) states four test questions and eight separately-numbered craft laws — it does not itemize a "six questions" set anywhere. The six below are my own synthesis, built by merging the four test questions with the most load-bearing craft laws (2 Want/Need, 3 Surrender, 4 wordless held beat, 6 catch-and-release, 7 bittersweet close) into a single usable per-episode checklist. Flagged as provisional pending Julian's actual definition — see the "note" field.

---

1) WHERE IS THE FEELING MADE VISIBLE RATHER THAN NARRATED?
Twice, both multisensory and wordless-first:
— Keen's father's WRISTBANDS begin a faint self-glow underwater in Scene 7 while he strains against the drift net ("A faint glow begins inside them. Soft. Steady.") — courage rendered as light on the body, not a line of dialogue about being brave. Canon's EP3 production notes confirm this is deliberately NOT crystal-powered yet — "courage from within, not the crystal."
— The Crystal Bears' combined beacon in Scene 6: Aida's Rose Quartz, then Misty's, Luna's, Howey's, Zenny's crystals pulse one by one, then "A brilliant BURST OF COMBINED LIGHT shoots out across the storm-dark sea" — collective care and hope made into a literal, visible-from-the-boat signal Keen can see and react to ("What is that…?").
— And the culminating instance: Scene 9's Aquamarine Crystal materializing, splitting into two gems, and settling into the (previously vacant) wristbands with "a pulse of AZURE LIGHT" Keen "feels it instantly" — the show's one true invention (feeling on the outside) landing exactly on the earned-crystal beat.

2) WHAT IS THE WANT VS THE NEED, EPISODE-WIDE? (craft law 2)
WANT (stated, performed, on the surface): Keen wants to prove he can do this alone — "I've got this, Mum" (Sc.3), "I've got this… I've got this…" repeated to himself mid-storm (Sc.4) and again bracing against the net (Sc.7), plus his running performance of confidence ("Still got it!" after nearly capsizing; the map-reading bravado he immediately fumbles). The WANT is competence performed solo — independence as self-sufficiency.
NEED (what the crystal/story actually resolves): Keen needs to learn that courage isn't going it alone — it's "part of growing up" that still means never being alone. His Mum names the real need twice before he can access it: "Wherever you go… you'll never be alone" (Sc.3) and Aida closes the loop verbatim in Sc.9: "That's what family does… Wherever you go, you'll never be alone." The gap between the WANT (I can do this by myself) and the NEED (courage is choosing to act FOR someone else, inside a net of people who have your back) is exactly where the dive for Squeaky lives — an unhesitating act of selflessness, not solo heroics, is what the crystal actually rewards.

3) WHERE IS THE ONE WORDLESS HELD BEAT (THE NADIR)? (craft law 4)
Scene 4, the moment the map is snatched by the wind and vanishes into the storm: "Keen stands frozen. Rainwater sloshes around his feet. The horizon disappears behind sheets of grey." This is the beat with no plan, no ally in frame (Squeaky has vanished beneath the water), and no crystal light yet — just Keen alone, small, and truly lost, immediately before "Oh no… I'm lost!!!" This is the true nadir: total disorientation, held a beat longer than comfortable, camera on his face in the downpour, before he has to self-soothe his way back ("I've got this… I've got this…"). It is the emotional low of the whole episode — the point every subsequent beat (Squeaky's rescue signal, the beacon, the dive, the crystal) resolves.

4) WHERE IS THE SURRENDER BEAT (NOT A POWER-UP)? (craft law 3)
Scene 7, underwater: Keen has already tried and failed to free Squeaky ("He grabs the net and pulls. Nothing… Still nothing. His eyes flick upward… He is running out of strength"). The turn is NOT a power surge — it's the moment he stops trying to force it alone and instead centers himself and simply commits fully: "He plants his feet against a submerged rock. Centers himself… I've got this… I've got this…" followed by the quiet escalation to "COME ON!" and the net finally tearing. Canon is explicit that this is deliberately pre-crystal (the wristbands are still "vacant," only self-glowing from courage, not crystal power) — the surrender is Keen letting the feeling (fear, exhaustion, the urge to save himself first) go and choosing Squeaky anyway, sincerity not volume. The ACTUAL Crystal Call/power-up doesn't fire until Scene 9, safely on shore, well AFTER the feeling has already been felt and acted on — exactly matching craft law 3's warning that a Call placed before the feeling is felt is the single biggest soul-violation. Here the order is correctly feeling → surrender → (much later) crystal.

5) WHERE IS THE CATCH-AND-RELEASE (FUNNY GOES QUIETLY TRUE, THEN EXITS WITHIN ~2S)? (craft law 6)
Scene 1: Fuzzby's comic pollen-moustache bit ("Do I look official?" / Zenny: "Yes Fuzzby. Officially nuts!") is pure gag-and-move-on — but the beat right after quietly turns true for half a second: a distant thunder RUMBLE, Fuzzby pauses mid-hover and says "…okay that sounded dramatic," Zenny looks to the sky and says, simply, "A Storm's coming" — a flicker of real unease inside the comedy — and it's released almost instantly: "Good thing I work well under pressure" / he immediately flies into a flower and gets stuck again, back to slapstick within roughly two lines. The lump (something's coming) and the laugh (he's stuck in a flower again) sit in the same breath, then the scene moves on without dwelling.
A second, later instance: Scene 9, Fuzzby "claps too early. Stops. Then starts clapping again louder to cover it" during Keen's Crystal Call — a half-second of vulnerable, true-to-character overeagerness inside the triumphant beat, immediately swallowed back into the group hug and comedy of Scene 10's honey riff.

6) WHERE IS THE BITTERSWEET CLOSE (THE ACHE RETAINED, NEVER RESET TO ZERO)? (craft law 7)
The episode's ending (Scene 10, the beach walk with the honey-riff comedy) is warm and light, but the ache it carries forward is Keen's father — never resolved away, only carried. The wristbands stay on Keen's wrists through the entire final act, explicitly named as continuity of loss and love: "I still feel him… every day," "Promise me you'll always wear them," and Keen's own "I won't be [alone]. I love you, Mum." By Scene 9 those same wristbands (once vacant, a physical reminder of an absence) now hold the Aquamarine crystal — the show doesn't erase the father's absence, it lets the crystal settle INTO the object that already carried that grief, so the joyful ending is warmer, but the trace of what it cost (a boy without his father, wearing his father's bands into every scene from here on) is still visibly on his body in the episode's very last images. It lands as an addition of belonging (the Bears, the crystal, the friendship with Fuzzby/Zenny) layered over an ache that is retained, not zeroed out — precisely the show's "never resolve the ache away" rule.
northStarNote: These six questions are my own synthesis (not an established, itemized "six" found anywhere in canon) — CRYSTAL_BEARS_LOCKED_CANON.md §0 only states four named test questions plus eight separately-numbered craft laws, so this framing is provisional pending Julian's actual definition of "the six."
